title: Walnut baklava
subtitle: Layers of filo pastry, walnuts, and honey syrup
excerpt: Walnut baklava is layers of buttered filo pastry packed with a spiced walnut filling, baked until deeply golden, then drenched with a honey and orange syrup while still hot. The syrup soaks into the filo over several hours as it cools, turning it from crisp to sticky and translucent. It keeps for several days at room temperature.
sourceNotes: Baklava is one of the oldest documented pastries in the Middle East and Eastern Mediterranean, with early references in Ottoman palace records from the fifteenth century and antecedents in earlier Persian and Arab sweet pastry traditions. Walnut baklava is particularly associated with Turkey and Greece; pistachio baklava with Syrian and Lebanese traditions. The technique of pouring cold syrup over hot baklava (or hot syrup over cold baklava) to achieve the right texture is consistent across regional versions and is the key step in home production.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Baklava improves with time. Make 1 to 2 days ahead and leave at room temperature, covered loosely.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 35 to 45 minutes until deep gold all the way through; cut into pieces before baking so syrup can penetrate

content.text: The filo must be buttered thoroughly between every layer: skimping here results in a dry, papery baklava rather than the rich, flaky result. Keep the filo sheets covered with a damp cloth as you work to prevent them drying out. The baklava is cut into diamonds or squares before it goes into the oven, not after: trying to cut through baked, soaked filo shatters the layers. The syrup is made cold and poured over the hot baklava the moment it comes from the oven.

items.prepNote: strip of peel and a squeeze of juice

content.text: Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Brush a 30 x 20 cm tin with melted butter. Mix the chopped walnuts with 50 g caster sugar and 1 tsp cinnamon. Trim the filo sheets to fit the tin. Lay 6 to 8 sheets of filo in the base, brushing each one generously with melted butter before adding the next. Spread half the walnut mixture evenly over the filo. Add 4 more buttered filo sheets, spread the remaining walnut mixture, then layer the remaining filo on top, buttering every sheet and the top layer thoroughly.

Using a sharp knife, cut the assembled baklava into diamonds or squares, cutting all the way through to the base. Bake for 35 to 45 minutes until deep gold all the way to the edges. Meanwhile, make the syrup: combine 200 g caster sugar, 150 ml water, 3 tablespoons of honey, and a strip of orange peel in a saucepan. Bring to a boil and simmer for 5 minutes. Squeeze in a little orange juice and remove from the heat. Leave to cool to room temperature. The moment the baklava comes from the oven, pour the cold syrup slowly and evenly all over it. Leave to absorb for at least 4 hours before serving.

items.problem: The baklava is dry and the filo is papery
items.solution: Not enough butter between the layers, or the syrup was not absorbed fully. Each sheet of filo must be brushed until lightly saturated, not just spotted. The syrup should be poured over while the baklava is still hot.

items.problem: The baklava is soggy rather than sticky and crisp
items.solution: Too much syrup or the syrup was hot when poured over cold baklava. The contrast of temperatures helps the baklava absorb the syrup without becoming wet. Pour cold syrup over hot baklava only.
